sectionName: Chemical & Physical Foundations of Biological Systems

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated a neuron-membrane analog circuit in which a resistor and capacitor modeled leakage conductance and membrane capacitance after a current pulse to connect a measurable physical trend with a the physical sciences principle. The central idea was that capacitor voltage changes exponentially and the time constant equals the product of resistance and capacitance. Before collecting data, the team identified the governing relationship as τ = RC and V(t) approaches its final value exponentially. This mattered because the same topic vocabulary could support several tempting but incompatible answers unless the variables, units, and controlled conditions were kept straight. The investigators therefore designed the experiment so that the figure had to be interpreted through proportionality, limiting behavior, and the direction of causality rather than through sentence recall alone.

The experimental manipulation involved increasing membrane resistance while holding capacitance constant and recording voltage recovery after identical pulses. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: higher resistance produced slower recovery and a larger measured time constant. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that capacitance, pulse amplitude, oscilloscope scaling, and temperature were controlled. Those controls were important because a change in a calibration setting, environmental condition, sample geometry, or hidden concentration could create an apparent trend without testing the intended physical relationship. A correct explanation must therefore account for both what was deliberately changed and what was explicitly held constant.

Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that raising resistance shortens the time constant because charge has a harder time leaking away. Another was that a capacitor reaches final voltage instantly as soon as current is applied. Each alternative contains a familiar phrase from chemistry or physics, so it could attract a student who recognizes a term but does not check whether the prediction matches the graph. The investigators rejected these explanations because they either reverse the expected direction, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore a conserved quantity, or attribute the effect to a controlled variable.

The follow-up analysis asked students to make a prediction from the same relationship. For this passage, the key is to connect the measured trend to membrane RC recovery, translate the graph into the appropriate mathematical dependency, and use the controls to rule out artifacts. The relevant calculation is intentionally modest; the harder part is deciding which variable belongs in the numerator, denominator, exponent, logarithm, or proportionality.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the membrane RC recovery experiment?

choices.A: Raising resistance shortens the time constant because charge has a harder time leaking away
choices.B: The slower recovery is best explained by the larger RC time constant when resistance is increased
choices.C: A capacitor reaches final voltage instantly as soon as current is applied
choices.D: The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls

explanations.A: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that Raising resistance shortens the time constant because charge has a harder time leaking away conflicts with the stated relationship, τ = RC and V(t) approaches its final value exponentially, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that higher resistance produced slower recovery and a larger measured time constant and states that capacitance, pulse amplitude, oscilloscope scaling, and temperature were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.B: Correct. This answer accounts for the manipulation, the plotted trend, and the controlled variables at the same time. The passage identifies the relevant relationship as τ = RC and V(t) approaches its final value exponentially, so the reasoning depends on how the variables are related rather than on a keyword match. In this experiment, higher resistance produced slower recovery and a larger measured time constant, while capacitance, pulse amplitude, oscilloscope scaling, and temperature were controlled. That pattern supports this choice because The slower recovery is best explained by the larger RC time constant when resistance is increased. It also predicts the correct follow-up behavior if the same variable is changed again. The tempting alternatives either reverse the dependency, mistake a controlled variable for the causal variable, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuse a measured signal with the physical quantity that produced it.
explanations.C: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that A capacitor reaches final voltage instantly as soon as current is applied conflicts with the stated relationship, τ = RC and V(t) approaches its final value exponentially, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that higher resistance produced slower recovery and a larger measured time constant and states that capacitance, pulse amplitude, oscilloscope scaling, and temperature were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.
explanations.D: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. The claim that The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls conflicts with the stated relationship, τ = RC and V(t) approaches its final value exponentially, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that higher resistance produced slower recovery and a larger measured time constant and states that capacitance, pulse amplitude, oscilloscope scaling, and temperature were controlled. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.

choices.C: The measured response changes only after a controlled variable is deliberately altered
choices.D: The plotted trend is opposite to the direction predicted by the stated relationship

questions.stem: Which broader Chemical and Physical Foundations principle is most directly illustrated by this experiment?

choices.A: Quantitative graphs should be interpreted without considering units or controlled variables
choices.B: A controlled variable is the most likely cause of a trend whenever it appears in an equation
choices.C: A memorized definition should override a measured trend when the two appear inconsistent
choices.D: RC circuits model membrane charging and discharging through exponential voltage changes

questions.passage:
Researchers investigated a defibrillator training circuit charged to different voltages before discharge through a standard test resistor to connect a measurable physical trend with a the physical sciences principle. The central idea was that energy stored in a capacitor is proportional to capacitance and to the square of voltage. Before collecting data, the team identified the governing relationship as U = 1/2 CV^2 and Q = CV. This mattered because the same topic vocabulary could support several tempting but incompatible answers unless the variables, units, and controlled conditions were kept straight. The investigators therefore designed the experiment so that the figure had to be interpreted through proportionality, limiting behavior, and the direction of causality rather than through sentence recall alone.

The experimental manipulation involved charging the same capacitor to increasing voltages while measuring delivered energy during discharge. Figure 1 summarizes the main result: delivered energy increased more than linearly as charging voltage increased. The investigators repeated the measurements after verifying that capacitance, discharge resistor, lead resistance, and waveform sampling were held constant. Those controls were important because a change in a calibration setting, environmental condition, sample geometry, or hidden concentration could create an apparent trend without testing the intended physical relationship. A correct explanation must therefore account for both what was deliberately changed and what was explicitly held constant.

Two alternative interpretations were considered. One was that capacitor energy is directly proportional to voltage rather than to voltage squared. Another was that increasing voltage reduces stored charge because the capacitor plates repel additional charge. Each alternative contains a familiar phrase from chemistry or physics, so it could attract a student who recognizes a term but does not check whether the prediction matches the graph. The investigators rejected these explanations because they either reverse the expected direction, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, ignore a conserved quantity, or attribute the effect to a controlled variable.

The follow-up analysis asked students to make a prediction from the same relationship. For this passage, the key is to connect the measured trend to defibrillator capacitor energy, translate the graph into the appropriate mathematical dependency, and use the controls to rule out artifacts. The relevant calculation is intentionally modest; the harder part is deciding which variable belongs in the numerator, denominator, exponent, logarithm, or proportionality.
questions.stem: Which interpretation best explains the main result in the defibrillator capacitor energy experiment?

choices.A: Capacitor energy is directly proportional to voltage rather than to voltage squared
choices.B: Increasing voltage reduces stored charge because the capacitor plates repel additional charge

choices.D: The pattern is most likely random noise despite the systematic dose-response trend and stated controls

The claim that The graph can be interpreted only by comparing the measured trend with the governing equation conflicts with the stated relationship, U = 1/2 CV^2 and Q = CV, or with the direction shown in the figure. The passage reports that delivered energy increased more than linearly as charging voltage increased and states that capacitance, discharge resistor, lead resistance, and waveform sampling were held constant. Those details make it inappropriate to explain the result by changing a variable that was controlled, reversing the predicted proportionality, or ignoring the units of the measured quantity. A stronger answer must connect the independent variable to the observed trend through the governing C/P principle.

questions.stem: Which broader Chemical and Physical Foundations principle is most directly illustrated by this experiment?

choices.A: Quantitative graphs should be interpreted without considering units or controlled variables
choices.B: A controlled variable is the most likely cause of a trend whenever it appears in an equation
choices.C: A memorized definition should override a measured trend when the two appear inconsistent
choices.D: Capacitors store electrical potential energy that can be released through a circuit element

explanations.A: This choice is based on a recognizable misconception, but it does not fit the complete experimental design. explanations.D: Correct. This answer accounts for the manipulation, the plotted trend, and the controlled variables at the same time. The passage identifies the relevant relationship as U = 1/2 CV^2 and Q = CV, so the reasoning depends on how the variables are related rather than on a keyword match. In this experiment, delivered energy increased more than linearly as charging voltage increased, while capacitance, discharge resistor, lead resistance, and waveform sampling were held constant. That pattern supports this choice because Capacitors store electrical potential energy that can be released through a circuit element. It also predicts the correct follow-up behavior if the same variable is changed again. The tempting alternatives either reverse the dependency, mistake a controlled variable for the causal variable, treat a nonlinear relationship as linear, or confuse a measured signal with the physical quantity that produced it.
